figures.caption: TABLE I: Cross-platform motion models and kinematic limits used in the hardware deployment experiments.

Fig. 11: Differential-drive dual-arm transportation in an indoor office environment. The numbered sequence shows passage through a narrow gate, pedestrian interaction in a narrow corridor, and final entry into a tight workspace for bottle placement; the full task trajectory is also shown.

Fig. 1: Overview of EXACT-MPPI, illustrating the four main contributions: (1) perception-to-control navigation from direct point clouds and weak waypoint guidance to motion commands; (2) analytic, exact signed-distance evaluation for 2D footprints, including non-convex polygons; (3) a GPU-parallel MPPI controller with JAX-compiled batched rollouts; and (4) cross-platform validation on differential-drive, omnidirectional, and hybrid robots in indoor and outdoor cluttered environments.
Fig. 9: IR-SIM comparison in the 8 m-wide corridor with mixed static and dynamic obstacles: (a) EXACT-MPPI, (b) Convex-MPPI, (c) Rectangle-MPPI, and (d) NeuPAN with a convex-hull T-shaped footprint.

Fig. 17: Extreme narrow-passage comparison for the Unitree Go2 with the carried bar: (a) elapsed traversal of EXACT-MPPI, (b) corresponding EXACT-MPPI planning result through the cluttered passage, and (c) Falco under the rectangular robot abstraction.
Fig. 8: Omni-directional gap scenario in the simulator at DoN = 1.05.

Fig. 7: Representative cases from the corridor DoN sweep: (a) NeuPAN at DoN = 0.6, (b) Convex-MPPI at DoN = 0.6, (c) NeuPAN at DoN = 1.0, and (d) EXACT-MPPI at DoN = 1.0. The zoomed inset in (d) shows how the explicit T-shaped footprint preserves a narrow feasible passage that is removed by the convex-hull approximation.
Fig. 19: Trajectory and commanded velocities for (a) hybrid-mode operation and (b) dual-Ackermann-only operation.

Fig. 18: Hybrid-mode navigation on AgileX Ranger mini. The full trajectory is reconstructed for visualization with FASTLIO2 [45]; local planning details show the point-cloud input, reference trajectory, sampled candidate paths, and selected MPPI trajectory at a representative replanning instant.
Fig. 15: Unitree Go2 garden deployment with the carried bar: overall trajectory and representative local obstacle-avoidance snapshots during the run.
Fig. 16: Falco in the same Unitree Go2 garden scenario with the carried bar.
Fig. 6: Inference time versus obstacle point count for DUNE and the JAX-based evaluator across four representative footprints.

Fig. 4: Representative signed-distance fields produced by the polygon-edge evaluator on six simple polygons of varying complexity. The gallery spans rectilinear (T, F), convex (Diamond, Trapezoid), and non-convex (Star, Arrow) footprints.
Fig. 12: Ranger mini trap scenario under parallel motion: (a) EXACT-MPPI with the explicit footprint representation and (b) Convex-MPPI with the convex-hull footprint representation.

Fig. 13: Ranger mini trap scenario under dual-Ackermann steering: (a) EXACT-MPPI, (b) Convex-MPPI, (c) NeuPAN with the nominal footprint configuration, and (d) NeuPAN with the added-load footprint configuration.
